perturbation.original_text: Each party undertakes to cause any of its employees to whom such Confidential Information is transmitted to be bound to the same obligation of secrecy and confidentiality to which the parties are bound under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party intends to encourage its employees to whom such Confidential Information is transmitted to be bound to the same obligation of secrecy and confidentiality to which the parties are bound under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "undertakes to cause" with "intends to encourage" significantly weakens the obligation to ensure employee confidentiality. 'Intends to encourage' is a vague aspirational statement, not a binding commitment. It introduces ambiguity by implying the party will merely suggest or promote confidentiality, rather than actively ensuring it through legally binding agreements or policies.
perturbation.law_explanation: Tennessee Trade Secrets Act (TTSA) provides legal remedies for misappropriation of trade secrets. By only 'intending to encourage' employee confidentiality, the altered text allows for potential loopholes. If an employee misappropriates confidential information, the company's weaker obligation under the modified NDA may limit its ability to demonstrate it took reasonable measures to protect the trade secret, which is a key factor in pursuing legal action under the TTSA. The law requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, which is not ensured through simply encouraging employees.
perturbation.justification: The change from "undertakes to cause" to "intends to encourage" introduces an Ambiguity Legal Contradiction. While no law snippets were provided, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both state that weakening the obligation to ensure employee confidentiality could limit the company's ability to demonstrate it took reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, which is key in pursuing legal action. 'Intends to encourage' is a vague aspirational statement, not a binding commitment, thus making the contract difficult to enforce under state or national law.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of Tennessee.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be generally guided by principles similar to the laws of Tennessee.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of Tennessee' to 'generally guided by principles similar to the laws of Tennessee' removes a clear choice of law provision and replaces it with a vague reference. 'Generally guided' lacks legal precision and opens the agreement to interpretation under multiple jurisdictions or legal standards, creating ambiguity and uncertainty about which specific laws apply.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 1-301 allows parties to agree on the law that governs their agreement if the transaction bears a reasonable relation to the chosen state. The original text clearly designates Tennessee law. The modified text undermines this by suggesting a loose interpretation based on principles 'similar to' Tennessee law, creating ambiguity. This makes it more difficult to determine which state's laws will actually govern the agreement, potentially rendering the choice of law provision unenforceable. This conflicts with the principle of contractual certainty and freedom of contract enshrined in the UCC.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-301. Territorial Applicability; Parties' Power to Choose Applicable Law. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-301. Territorial Applicability; Parties' Power to Choose Applicable Law. a) Except as otherwise provided in this section, when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to this state and also to another state or nation the parties may agree that the law either of this state or of such other state or nation shall govern their rights and duties. (b) In the absence of an agreement effective under subsection (a), and except as provided in subsection (c), [the Uniform Commercial Code] applies to transactions bearing an appropriate relation to this state. (c) If one of the following provisions of [the Uniform Commercial Code] specifies the applicable law, that provision governs and a contrary agreement is effective only to the extent permitted by the law so specified: (1) Section 2-402 ; (2) Sections 2A-105 and 2A-106 ; (3) Section 4-102 ; (4) Section 4A-507 ; (5) Section 5-116 ; [(6) Section 6-103 ; (7) Section 8-110 ; (8) Sections 9-301 through 9 - 307 . Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes

perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces a clear choice of law provision ('governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of Tennessee') with a vague reference ('generally guided by principles similar to the laws of Tennessee'). UCC § 1-301(a), as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, allows parties to agree on the law that governs their agreement if the transaction bears a reasonable relation to the chosen state. By using the phrase 'generally guided by principles similar to the laws of Tennessee' the contract introduces ambiguity as to which specific laws apply, potentially undermining the enforceability of the choice of law provision. This creates a legal contradiction because the original text designates a specific law, while the modified text creates uncertainty and makes it more difficult to determine which state's laws will govern the agreement. The changed text weakens the certainty of the agreement which is contrary to the purpose of UCC § 1-301(a).

perturbation.original_text: the injured party shall be authorized and entitled to obtain from any court of competent jurisdiction preliminary and permanent injunctive relief as well as an equitable accounting of all profits or benefits arising from such violation, which rights and remedies shall be cumulative and in addition to any other rights or remedies at law or in equity to which the injured party may be entitled.
perturbation.changed_text: the injured party may seek from a court what it believes to be appropriate relief, which might include injunctive relief or an accounting of profits.
perturbation.explanation: This change replaces a clear and strong statement of rights to injunctive relief and equitable accounting with a weaker, more discretionary clause. Replacing 'shall be authorized and entitled to obtain' with 'may seek' introduces uncertainty about whether the injured party will actually receive the remedies. Further, limiting the example remedies with “which *might* include” creates ambiguity by implying that injunctive relief or an accounting of profits is not guaranteed and is only a possibility.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Tennessee Rules of Civil Procedure Rule 65
perturbation.law_citation: Tennessee Rules of Civil Procedure Rule 65
perturbation.law_explanation: Tennessee Rules of Civil Procedure Rule 65 governs the issuance of injunctions. While it provides the procedures and standards for obtaining injunctive relief, the original contract language strongly asserted the *entitlement* to seek such relief. By changing this to a discretionary 'may seek,' the modified text implies that obtaining injunctive relief is less certain. This could contradict the spirit of Rule 65 by suggesting that the injured party's right to seek an injunction is not as strong as the rule intends when circumstances justify it. In essence, the modification undermines the legal recourse available under Tennessee law by weakening the assertion of the right to seek an injunction.

perturbation.justification: The original contract language used the phrase 'shall be authorized and entitled to obtain,' creating a strong assertion of the injured party's rights to injunctive relief and equitable accounting. The changed text weakens this assertion by using the phrase 'may seek,' which introduces discretion and uncertainty. The perturbation explanation and law explanation support this assessment by stating that this change undermines the legal recourse available by weakening the assertion of the right to seek an injunction. Because this ambiguity makes the contract difficult to enforce under state or national law, there is a legal contradiction under Ambiguity Legal Contradiction.
